incredible-rise-and-fall-of-the-talk-t=
uah-podcast?utm_campaign=3DWhat+I%27m+Hearing+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F16%2F25%=
29&utm_content=3DWhat+I%27m+Hearing+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F13%2F25%29&utm_med=
ium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a )=
]

=E2=80=8A

Scary
Streaming Numbers=E2=80=A6
--------------------

With the exception of Peacock, the major streaming services are all profita=
ble now, their owners will
delight in telling you. But all is far from well in the streaming business,=
 as a new MoffettNathanson report breaks down. Some highlights:

* Viewership is slowing: =E2=80=9CSVOD engagement growth in the U.S. is sta=
lling, and we do not forecast content investments to ramp up anywhere meani=
ngfully enough to change that trajectory.=E2=80=9D
* YouTube is gaining: =E2=80=9CSVOD=E2=80=99s share of total television usa=
ge was flat in each quarter of 2024. Rather, streaming growth has really co=
me from two places: YouTube and AVOD/FAST (Tubi, PlutoTV and Roku Channel).=
=E2=80=9D
* Netflix,
with huge scale and engagement, also provides users more bang for their buc=
k: =E2=80=9CNetflix in 2024 generated less revenue per hour viewed in the U=
=2ES. than any other major SVOD platfor=
m at just $0.36, of which only $0.34 c=
ame out of its users=E2=80=99 pockets in the form of subscription fees.=E2=
=80=9D Lucas Shaw
asked ( https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2025-02-16/spotify-wants=
-to-sell-you-concert-tickets?utm_campaign=3DWhat+I%27m+Hearing+-+SUBSCRIBER=
S+%282%2F16%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWhat+I%27m+Hearing+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F1=
3%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c=
60617da42abd12a ), Is Netflix underpriced?
* Merge or die: =E2=80=9CThe economics of streaming do not seem to work at =
the current scale of Peacock or Paramount+. They just might work if the two=
 came together or in
combination with Max.=E2=80=9D

=E2=80=8A

The
Feedback
--------

My =E2=80=98SNL50=E2=80=99 report and Thursday=E2=80=99s somewhat fiery cha=
t with Netflix content chief Bela Bajaria generated
lots of feedback=E2=80=A6

=E2=80=9CThis is how you interview a major entertainment executive. You wer=
e respectful, but also applied an appropriate amount of pressure. I wish we=
 lived in a world in which just one media exec could acknowledge the realit=
y of the current ecosystem while still serving their corporate-mandated P.R=
=2E spin. It would be far more endeari=
ng than this head-in-sand denialism.=E2=
=80=9D =E2=80=94An analyst

=E2=80=9CI like [Bajaria] more
now. I admire when executives take hard questions and are willing to give i=
t right back. You two should go on the road together.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94An =
agent

=E2=80=9CAmazing to see Bajaria claim Oppenheimer would have been just as s=
uccessful on Netflix when her evidence is Red Notice, a giant burning pile =
of money that no one cares about or remembers. How stupid do they think we =
are?=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94A journalist

=E2=80=9CDoes SNL50 kind of feel like it has this
undertone as a farewell to democracy?=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94An executive

Now, here=E2=80=99s Scott=E2=80=99s interview with the head of Imax=E2=80=
=99s China operation=E2=80=A6

Big in China

***************************************************************************=
***************************************************************************=
***************************************************************************=
*********************************
Big in China ( https://puck.news/imax-is-big-in-china/?utm_campaign=3DWhat+=
I%27m+Hearing+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F16%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWhat+I%27m+Hear=
ing+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F13%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3D=

One territory accounts for almost half of Imax=E2=80=99s global footprint,
and it helped drive the world=E2=80=99s first single-market billion-dollar =
blockbuster. In this revealing conversation, Imax China C.E.O. Daniel Manwa=
ring explains the recent jolt and what it might mean for Hollywood this sum=
mer.

Scott Mendelson
Scott Mendelson ( https://puck.news/author/scott-mendelson/?utm_campaign=3D=
What+I%27m+Hearing+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F16%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWhat+I%27m=
+Hearing+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F13%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_sour=
ce=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a )

No film has ever earned $1 billion at the U.S. box office. Avatar didn=E2=
=80=99t do it, nor did Star
Wars: The Force Awakens or Avengers: Endgame. But the North American market=
 is nowhere near as large as China, where Ne Zha 2, a follow-up to the 2019=
 animated blockbuster, has now grossed $1.636 billion and counting since it=
s release on January 29=E2=80=94crushing the previous global record for a s=
ingle marketplace. Any day now, Ne Zha 2 will also become the first non-Eng=
lish-language film to crack the top 10 of all-time worldwide theatrical gro=
ssers. It=E2=80=99s also
not alone. During the past three weeks, which included the Lunar New Year, =
Chinese audiences turned out for Detective Chinatown 1900 ($440 million), C=
reation of the Gods II ($160 million), and Boonie Bears: Future Reborn (jus=
t over $100 million). Combined, these four blockbusters have grossed more t=
han $2 billion in less than a month.

This massive haul can be attributed, in part, to Imax screens, which are av=
ailable in nearly 800 Chinese theaters=E2=80=94nearly half of
Imax=E2=80=99s global footprint. The Lunar New Year tentpoles earned $121 m=
illion from Imax alone, nearly double the $66 million record set during the=
 same window in 2023. To find out why, I called up Daniel Manwaring, C.E.O.=
 of Imax China, to break it all down. Our conversation has been edited for =
length and clarity.

The Post-Quota Era
------------------

Scott Mendelson: What explains the success of Ne
Zha 2? Is it essentially a Shrek 2 phenomenon=E2=80=94namely, a well-liked =
sequel to a beloved original=E2=80=94or is there more to it?

Daniel Manwaring: Ne Zha comes from a much larger universe called Investitu=
re of the Gods. It=E2=80=99s a small piece of a story that runs thousands o=
f years, and which children learn growing up. So you=E2=80=99ve got I.P. th=
at
has been instilled in people for so long. Then you combine it with the fact=
 that it=E2=80=99s a good movie. It=E2=80=99s got laughter, amazing action =
sequences, and the best animation quality China has ever done. It hits ever=
y demographic. Moreover, we=E2=80=99ve seen a lot of Chinese films that are=
 partially about teaching the audience something, often with a very on-the-=
nose message. Ne Zha 2 doesn=E2=80=99t have any of that.

Most Hollywood films released in China this decade have earned less than
expected. One exception has been films from 20th Century Studios. For examp=
le, Alien: Romulus topped $100 million in China last summer. And Free Guy n=
abbed $95 million in 2021.

Alien: Romulus would have likely never gotten into China until somewhat rec=
ently. That was an edgy film for this market. One reason it did so well is =
that a mother took her child
to the film, the kid had a panic attack, and it was posted on social media.=
 It inspired people to want to go see the film. I think those genres, which=
 includes the Fox I.P., are a little edgier and a little more violent. We=
=E2=80=99re seeing a little opening on the threshold of censorship.

Anora ( https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3DEMTHwp1UPjk&utm_campaign=3DWhat+I=

%27m+Hearing+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F16%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWhat+I%27m+Heari=
ng+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F13%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dc=

ANORA IS THE MOST AWARDED FILM OF THE YEAR.

=C2=A0

Winner of the Critics Choice Best Picture, DGA Best Director, and PGA Best =
Picture awards, ANORA has been hailed as =E2=80=9Ca marvel of filmmaking an=
d acting=E2=80=9D (NYT) that =E2=80=9Creaffirms that movies are still capab=
le of generating miracles=E2=80=9D (Rolling Stone).=C2=A0

=C2=A0

A culmination of 30 years of filmmaking from writer/director/editor
Sean Baker, ANORA is nominated for six Academy Awards=C2=AE including Best =
Picture and Best Director.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

This awards season, the choice is simple:=C2=A0

Follow your heart.=C2=A0

So we might see a push toward R-rated genre films that aren=E2=80=99t just =
patriotic or nationalist war
epics?

One-hundred percent. Deadpool & Wolverine and Alien: Romulus had blood ever=
ywhere, heads exploding, and yet they got in with few edits. This current a=
dministration of the Film Bureau is focused on box office performance. The =
attitude in China is: We=E2=80=99re open for business. I haven=E2=80=99t ev=
en heard the word =E2=80=9Cquota=E2=80=9D in like, two years. I don=E2=80=
=99t even know if anyone=E2=80=99s keeping count of the quota anymore. [Ed.
note: The Chinese government caps the number of foreign films that can be r=
eleased each year; it started at 10 but has since been raised to 34.]

The Post-Covid
Recovery
--------------

Covid arguably hit the Chinese movie business harder than Hollywood. In som=
e ways, it=E2=80=99s still
recovering.

China was essentially late to the post-Covid world. They basically delayed =
everything for a year. So in 2023, you had a pipeline of about three years =
of films that were on a shelf. The Film Bureau came in, they turned on the =
faucet, and everything flooded out in 2023. However, in 2024, you had many =
if not most of the films launching that year that were produced amid this p=
ost-Covid hangover. People didn=E2=80=99t know what the budget should
be, or if audiences were going to go back to the movies. You had this trans=
itional kind of year. There just really weren=E2=80=99t any big movies. But=
 now Ne Zha 2 just passed $104 million in Chinese Imax auditoriums alone, p=
ast the previous milestone, Avengers: Endgame, which earned around $83 mill=
ion in 2019.

We=E2=80=99ve heard bullish rumblings on the live-action How to Train Your =
Dragon remake=E2=80=94the previous two did about $60 million
each in China. I=E2=80=99m curious if the franchises whose previous install=
ments took downturns in China, like Mission: Impossible and Jurassic World,=
 or even the MCU flicks, can get back to 2010s-era earnings this summer.

It comes down to, =E2=80=9CHow relatable is the film?=E2=80=9D Chinese audi=
ences got burnt out on the same formula over and over. The timing of Oppenh=
eimer
[which earned $61 million in the market] affected the Imax release of Missi=
on: Impossible=E2=80=94Dead Reckoning globally.

What are the trouble signs you see for China?

If you had asked me that question last year, I would have had a laundry lis=
t of things to say. However, NRG did a global survey, and 35 percent of the=
 United States is willing to go see a movie in a theater. That matched up w=
ith the U.K. and
France and most of Europe. China was 78 percent. I was telling my board, =
=E2=80=9CI know things feel dim, but just trust this number.=E2=80=9D

There are still some risks: Does the film supply remain strong? Do release =
dates get pushed? Do we see people say, =E2=80=9COh, I don't want to releas=
e this summer. I want to wait until the next Chinese New Year=E2=80=9D? How=
ever, almost 300 million people are going to movie theaters here. As for us=
, specifically, as of this past Sunday, around 11.28 million admissions wer=
e
recorded for Ne Zha 2 in Imax in China. That=E2=80=99s just a crazy number.

What should Hollywood studios take away from the recent surge in moviegoing=
 in China?

It=E2=80=99s a great opportunity. Moviegoing in China tends to yield more m=
oviegoing; it=E2=80=99s a consumer behavior that builds on itself. The Film=
 Bureau designed this Chinese New Year slate to give the Chinese box office=
 a jolt of adrenaline, and they=E2=80=99ve clearly done an incredible
job. So, the hope is that will help buoy both the strong Chinese local-lang=
uage slate and the Hollywood slate ahead in 2025. I=E2=80=99d encourage U.S=
=2E studios to lean into this opportuni=
ty with marketing campaigns that caref=
ully consider local preferences and cultural nuances, and feel vital and re=
sonant. Chinese audiences aren=E2=80=99t all that different from those in t=
he U.S.=E2=80=94they want fresh, innovative storytelling that they haven=E2=
=80=99t seen or experienced before.

=E2=80=8A

Finally=E2=80=A6
----------

Disney=E2=80=99s Thunderbolts may have won the Super Bowl ad derby, given i=
ts spike in =E2=80=9Cunaided
awareness=E2=80=9D this week, according to The Quorum. Other winners includ=
e Jurassic World: Rebirth, which jumped from 2 percent unaided last week to=
 5 percent, and Lilo & Stitch and Mission: Impossible=E2=80=94The Final Rec=
koning, both of which rose to 4 percent=E2=80=A6

 do it, nor did <em>Star
Wars: The Force Awakens </em>or <em>Avengers: Endgame</em>. But the North A=
merican market is nowhere near as large as China, where <em>Ne Zha 2, </em>=
a follow-up to the 2019 animated blockbuster, has now grossed <em>$1.636 bi=
llion</em> and counting since its release on January 29=E2=80=94crushing th=
e previous global record for a single marketplace. Any day now, <em>Ne Zha =
2</em> will also become the first non-English-language film to crack the to=
p 10 of all-time worldwide theatrical grossers. It=E2=80=99s also
not alone. During the past three weeks, which included the Lunar New Year, =
Chinese audiences turned out for <em>Detective Chinatown 1900 </em>($440 mi=
llion), <em>Creation of the Gods II </em>($160 million), and <em>Boonie Bea=
rs: Future Reborn</em> (just over $100 million). Combined, these four block=
busters have grossed more than $2 billion in less than a month.<br/><br/>Th=
is massive haul can be attributed, in part, to Imax screens, which are avai=
lable in nearly 800 Chinese theaters=E2=80=94nearly half of
Imax=E2=80=99s global footprint. The Lunar New Year tentpoles earned $121 m=
illion from Imax alone, nearly double the $66 million record set during the=
 same window in 2023. To find out why, I called up <strong>Daniel Manwaring=
</strong>, C.E.O. of Imax China, to break it all down. Our conversation has=
 been edited for length and clarity.</p></div>

 that it=E2=80=99s a good movie. It=E2=80=99s got laughter, amazing action =
sequences, and the best animation quality China has ever done. It hits ever=
y demographic. Moreover, we=E2=80=99ve seen a lot of Chinese films that are=
 partially about teaching the audience something, often with a very on-the-=

<strong>Most Hollywood films released in China this decade have earned less=
 than

em>Free Guy </em>nabbed $95 million in 2021.</strong><br/><br/><em>Alien: R=
omulus</em> would have likely never gotten into China until somewhat recent=
ly. That was an edgy film for this market. One reason it did so well is tha=
t a mother took her child
to the film, the kid had a panic attack, and it was posted on social media.=
 It inspired people to want to go see the film. I think those genres, which=
 includes the Fox I.P., are a little edgier and a little more violent. We=
=E2=80=99re seeing a little opening on the threshold of censorship.</p></di=

dth:100%;"><h3 style=3D"margin:0;color:#000000;direction:ltr;font-family:&#=
39;PuckSerifDisplay','Georgia';font-size:24px;font-weight:400;l=
etter-spacing:normal;line-height:120%;text-align:center;margin-top:0;margin=
-bottom:0;mso-line-height-alt:28.799999999999997px;"><strong>The Post-Covid
Recovery</strong></h3>

<table width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" role=
=3D"presentation" style=3D"mso-table-lspace:0pt;mso-table-rspace:0pt;word-b=
reak:break-word;"><tbody><tr><td style=3D"padding-bottom:32px;"><div style=
=3D"color:#000000;direction:ltr;font-family:'Inter','Arial'=
;font-size:17px;font-weight:400;letter-spacing:0px;line-height:150%;text-al=
ign:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"margin:0;"><strong>Covid =
arguably hit the Chinese movie business harder than Hollywood. In some ways=
, it=E2=80=99s still
recovering.</strong><br/><br/>China was essentially late to the post-Covid =
world. They basically delayed everything for a year. So in 2023, you had a =
pipeline of about three years of films that were on a shelf. The Film Burea=
u came in, they turned on the faucet, and everything flooded out in 2023. H=
owever, in 2024, you had many if not most of the films launching that year =
that were produced amid this post-Covid hangover. People didn=E2=80=99t kno=
w what the budget should
be, or if audiences were going to go back to the movies. You had this trans=
itional kind of year. There just really weren=E2=80=99t any big movies. But=
 now <em>Ne Zha 2 </em>just passed $104 million in Chinese Imax auditoriums=
 alone, past the previous milestone, <em>Avengers: Endgame</em>, which earn=
ed around $83 million in 2019.<br/><br/><strong>We=E2=80=99ve heard bullish=
 rumblings on the live-action <em>How to Train Your Dragon </em>remake=E2=
=80=94the previous two did about $60 million
each in China. I=E2=80=99m curious if the franchises whose previous install=
ments took downturns in China, like <em>Mission: Impossible </em>and <em>Ju=
rassic World</em>, or even the MCU flicks, can get back to 2010s-era earnin=
gs this summer.</strong><br/><br/>It comes down to, =E2=80=9CHow relatable =
is the film?=E2=80=9D Chinese audiences got burnt out on the same formula o=
ver and over. The timing of <em>Oppenheimer
</em>[which earned $61 million in the market] affected the Imax release of =
<em>Mission: Impossible</em>=E2=80=94<em>Dead Reckoning </em>globally.<br/>=
<br/><strong>What are the trouble signs you see for China?</strong><br/><br=
/>If you had asked me that question last year, I would have had a laundry l=
ist of things to say. However, NRG did a global survey, and 35 percent of t=
he United States is willing to go see a movie in a theater. That matched up=
 with the U.K. and
France and most of Europe. China was 78 percent. I was telling my board, =
=E2=80=9CI know things feel dim, but just trust this number.=E2=80=9D<br/><=
br/>There are still some risks: Does the film supply remain strong? Do rele=
ase dates get pushed? Do we see people say, =E2=80=9COh, I don't want t=
o release this summer. I want to wait until the next Chinese New Year=E2=80=
=9D? However, almost 300 million people are going to movie theaters here. A=
s for us, specifically, as of this past Sunday, around 11.28 million admiss=
ions were
recorded for <em>Ne Zha 2 </em>in Imax in China. That=E2=80=99s just a craz=
y number.<br/><br/><strong>What should Hollywood studios take away from the=
 recent surge in moviegoing in China?</strong><br/><br/>It=E2=80=99s a grea=
t opportunity. Moviegoing in China tends to yield more moviegoing; it=E2=80=
=99s a consumer behavior that builds on itself. The Film Bureau designed th=
is Chinese New Year slate to give the Chinese box office a jolt of adrenali=
ne, and they=E2=80=99ve clearly done an incredible
job. So, the hope is that will help buoy both the strong Chinese local-lang=
uage slate and the Hollywood slate ahead in 2025. I=E2=80=99d encourage U.S=
=2E studios to lean into this opportuni=
ty with marketing campaigns that caref=
ully consider local preferences and cultural nuances, and feel vital and re=
sonant. Chinese audiences aren=E2=80=99t all that different from those in t=
he U.S.=E2=80=94they want fresh, innovative storytelling that they haven=E2=
=80=99t seen or experienced before.</p></div>
</td></tr></tbody></table>
